name: Architecture Proposal: Minimal Kernel & Hob Runtime

description.s: Architecture Proposal: Minimal Kernel & Hob Runtime

This proposal restructures Hobson into three clean layers: a minimal JS kernel providing only storage, modules, and events; the Hob interpreter as the system's programming model; and everything else — rendering, navigation, selection, error handling, UI — written in Hob. The goal is a system that describes itself in its own language, where dependencies are explicit and structurally visible, and where the maximum amount of code is inspectable and modifiable from within the system.
The current architecture has evolved organically. kernel-core is ~2500 lines containing the EventBus, the Kernel class, a ~750-line

method, import/export logic, UI fallbacks (item palette, help dialog, raw editor), and declarative watch dispatch. The rendering system is another ~1100 lines inside the kernel. The kernel hardcodes knowledge of specific userland libraries (viewport-manager, selection-manager) by name.

Meanwhile, Hob — the system's own language — is used only for views. System-level libraries (viewport-manager, selection-manager, keyboard-shortcuts, repl-ui, error handling, etc.) are all raw JS. This means the most architecturally significant code in the system is the least inspectable.
The proposal addresses both problems: shrink the kernel to its irreducible core, and use Hob for everything above it.
The kernel provides the minimum needed to make items-with-dependencies work. Nothing more.
— CRUD operations on items. Type-chain validation. Cycle detection. Owns IndexedDB access and ID prefixing for nested instances.
loads code items. For JS items: Blob URL → dynamic import → cached exports. For Hob items: fetch AST from storage, evaluate in a child environment, return local
bindings as a map. Cache management and invalidation on code item changes.
— Subscribe, emit, hierarchical type dispatch. Declarative watch setup (scanning items for
field, dispatching events to matching handlers). This is the mechanism through which userland activates at boot.
— Initialize storage, ensure seed items, build event type cache, setup watches, emit

. Provide a DOM root node. If no watcher takes over the DOM (because userland is broken), the kernel's natural state is a basic REPL and raw JSON display — which is safe mode, emerging naturally rather than as a special path.

— The kernel provides a bare-bones REPL (eval input, show output) as part of its fallback state. This is distinct from the full-featured REPL (syntax highlighting, history, autocomplete, UI chrome) which is a Layer 3 service. The kernel REPL is deliberately minimal — just enough to inspect items, evaluate expressions, and recover from a broken Layer 3.
— Catch uncaught errors and promise rejections, emit
events. The kernel does not handle errors itself (no toast, no error items) — it just emits the event. A userland watcher handles the rest.
. That's the complete surface. No rendering, no navigation, no viewport, no attach/detach, no import/export.
Layer 2: Hob (JS interpreter, first thing loaded)
The Hob interpreter is necessarily JS — it can't be written in the language it interprets. But it's the only JS at this level. Everything above it is Hob.
Hob's role is not just "view language" but "the system's programming model." It provides:

— Code remains as AST throughout its lifecycle. Before require, it's an AST in storage. After require, the interpreter has evaluated it, but the AST is still there in the item. Nothing is compiled away or hidden.
— Atoms with automatic dependency tracking. When an atom mutates, the system knows which rendered contexts depend on it and can re-render them.
— Declarative DOM construction from data structures.
is called on a Hob item, the interpreter evaluates the AST in a fresh child environment. The result is a map of the module's local
provides publicity. No export declarations needed.
— Since all Hob code is AST (data), the system can walk it to find dependencies (
calls), item references, and more — without executing the code. Tooling written in Hob can operate on Hob.

— The interpreter is the single point of failure for all of Layer 3. If it fails to load or throws during evaluation, the kernel falls back to JS-based raw views for the broken items and the safe mode REPL. This keeps the system recoverable without requiring the interpreter to be perfect, and allows the interpreter to be iterated on aggressively.
All system-level concerns become Hob modules, orchestrated by the viewport manager.
. Takes ownership of the kernel's DOM root, creates the layout structure (DOM containers), then explicitly initializes other services in the right order via
. This makes the dependency graph fully explicit and structurally visible — no implicit coordination, no priority system, no independent actors racing to attach.

— Required by the viewport manager. Handles view resolution (walking type chains), render instance registry, partial re-render, the Hob view framework. If the Hob interpreter fails, falls back to JS-based raw view rendering.
— Required by the viewport manager. Tracks which item is selected and in which parent context.
— Required by the viewport manager. Binds all keyboard shortcuts. (Only Ctrl+Shift+S for safe mode remains hardcoded in the kernel.)
— Required by the viewport manager. Creates and manages the full-featured REPL UI (syntax highlighting, history, autocomplete). Distinct from the kernel's bare-bones safe mode REPL.
. Creates error items, shows toast notifications.
— A library. Contains the item-type-aware refresh logic that currently lives in kernel-core's
Item palette, help dialog, raw editor
— Views or libraries. The kernel fallbacks for these are removed (the safe mode REPL covers the recovery use case).
— A library required by the viewport manager that injects CSS, rather than being a kernel boot step.
All views, field views, type definitions
This is the key mechanism that makes Hob modules possible.
The module system fetches the item's AST from storage (the
The interpreter creates a child environment (parent = root environment with stdlib)
Each expression in the AST is evaluated in this child environment
forms add bindings to the child environment's local scope
The local bindings (not inherited stdlib) are returned as a Hob map
for private. No export declarations, no metadata, no new concepts.

;; In viewport-manager module:
(let [internal-state (atom nil)          ;; private
      parse-url (fn [] ...)]             ;; private
  (def navigate (fn [id] ...))           ;; public
  (def get-root (fn [] ...)))            ;; public
A future ergonomic improvement: destructuring require, e.g.
, to reduce the friction of the

The system's dependency graph, event flow, and module structure are all visible as data. A system browser can walk Hob ASTs to show what requires what, what subscribes to which events, what types a view handles. No string parsing, no heuristics — structural queries on structured data.

Today, kernel-core is an opaque 95KB blob. The rendering system is 40KB of opaque JS. In this model, the kernel shrinks to ~1000 lines of truly irreducible infrastructure, and everything else is inspectable Hob that the user can open, read, understand, and modify.

Today, system-level libraries are JS with ad-hoc patterns. Views are Hob with reactive state. Watchers are JS with convention-based handler names. In this model, everything speaks the same language with the same patterns for state (atoms), dependencies (require), and lifecycle (watches).
. But the dependency is right there. You can see it. The system can see it. There's no magic global, no kernel method hiding where the call actually goes.
Currently kernel methods. But the kernel doesn't traverse or interpret the attachment graph for its own purposes — rendering does. These methods are sugar over
. They could be a Hob library. The one wrinkle is
cleaning up parent references, but a delete-event watcher handles that.
— Opens IndexedDB, loads kernel-core, passes db + instanceId to kernel, calls
— Initializes storage, ensures seeds, builds event type cache, sets up declarative watch dispatch
listener. Takes ownership of the DOM root, creates layout containers, requires and initializes services in order: rendering system, selection manager, keyboard shortcuts, REPL, styles
— Loads the Hob interpreter (its dependency), renders the root item

If step 4 or 5 fails (broken viewport or rendering code), the kernel's DOM root shows its safe mode state: the basic REPL and raw JSON display. The user can fix things via that REPL or safe mode (Ctrl+Shift+S).
This is a significant restructuring. A staged approach:
Extract import/export, raw editor, item palette fallback, and help dialog fallback out of kernel-core into separate items. Remove hardcoded
to pure kernel primitives. This can be done independently and tested against existing userland.
Extend the module system so that requiring a Hob item evaluates its AST in a child environment and returns local bindings as a map. This is the foundational capability for Hob modules.
Stage 3: Move rendering out of the kernel.

The rendering system becomes a Hob module (or a set of Hob modules) required by the viewport manager. This interleaves with the nested rendering work — the new rendering approach would be built outside the kernel from the start.
Stage 4: Port JS libraries to Hob.

Starting with simpler ones (selection-manager, keyboard-shortcuts) and working up to viewport-manager and repl-ui. Each port is independent and testable. Profile hot-path performance before committing — keyboard dispatch and selection tracking need sub-frame response times. Some services may need to remain JS if async overhead is prohibitive.

As functionality moves to Hob, the kernel shrinks. Eventually kernel-core is just: boot sequence, storage composition, module system composition, event bus, watch dispatch, safe mode REPL, and a minimal base API.

The interpreter is currently fast enough for views. Will it be fast enough for system-level code that runs on every event? The async-everything nature of Hob (every function returns a Promise) is the real constraint. Keyboard shortcut dispatch, selection management, and event routing are hot paths that run synchronously on every interaction. The interop pitfalls already known (Hob fns can't be used as synchronous callbacks) would apply to these system-level services too. Profiling is needed before committing to porting hot-path libraries. Some services may need to stay JS, or Hob may need a synchronous evaluation mode.
Some things may always need to be JS (e.g., interfacing with CodeMirror, markdown parsers, other JS libraries loaded from CDN). Hob already has
for calling JS methods on objects. The question is whether Hob modules can cleanly wrap JS libraries, or whether some items need to remain JS.

At ~3200 lines and 109KB, the interpreter is the largest single item. It's necessarily JS. Under this proposal it becomes the single point of failure for all of Layer 3 — a bug that currently breaks only views would now break the entire system. The JS view fallback path and safe mode REPL mitigate this, but the interpreter's reliability and debuggability matter more in this architecture. Should it remain a single monolithic item, or be decomposed?
All five migration stages are complete:
) is the rendering engine; kernel-core no longer contains rendering logic.
— Done. Hob views are the primary authoring model; the interpreter evaluates Hob code and morphdom diffs the result.
) handles navigation as a userland library.
— Done. Selection is managed by the rendering system, not the kernel.
— Done. The kernel provides storage, modules, and events; everything else is userland.
The architecture described in this proposal is now the live system.
